_work.title: Conjuration Circles & the Necromantic Tradition
_work.creator: various
_work.blurb: The conjuration circle — a bounded zone of geometric and epigraphic power — is the defining visual object of the Western grimoire tradition. Inscribed with divine names in Latin, Hebrew, and Greek, divided by pentagrams, hexagrams, and crosses, the circle both protected the conjurer from the spirit being summoned and fixed the ritual space's sanctity. Here is the full range: the elegant protective circles of the *Clavicula Salomonis* manuscripts (British Library, Lansdowne and Harley collections), the rougher working diagrams of the Munich Necromancer's Manual, the elaborate *Sigillum Dei* of the *Sworn Book of Honorius*, Faust's infamous conjuration in popular illustration, and John Dee's *Holy Table* and *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* — the last translation of the medieval circle into an Elizabethan cosmological instrument.

entries.title: The Goetic Circle, *Lesser Key of Solomon* (Mathers–Crowley edition)
entries.creator: Samuel Liddell MacGregor Mathers and Aleister Crowley
entries.summary:
The Goetic Circle as printed in Mathers and Crowley's landmark 1904 edition of *The Goetia* (Book I of the *Lemegeton Clavicula Salomonis*).

## Iconography
The double ring is densely packed with divine and angelic names arranged at the cardinal and intercardinal points, the whole girded by a third outer ring. A pentagram is inscribed in the centre and an equilateral triangle extends outside the circle at the south — the Triangle of Art into which the spirit is commanded to appear visible. Cross-marks divide the annular bands. The operator stands inside; the spirit is confined to the triangle.

## Significance
This is the canonical conjuration apparatus of the Goetic tradition. The *Lemegeton* compiles and systematises earlier English Solomonic manuscripts into a definitive instrument of spirit-working, and this circle is the operator's sanctified ground for all seventy-two operations it describes. The divine names inscribed — drawn from Hebrew scripture, the Psalms, and angelic nomenclature — theoretically compel any spirit to come, appear, and speak truthfully within the triangle. Mathers and Crowley's 1904 edition standardised the diagrammatic conventions that all subsequent ceremonial magic inherits.

## For artists & game designers
The Circle-plus-Triangle layout is the archetypal 'summoning circle' design: two concentric rings for the operator, a geometric extrusion for the entity. Copy this structure directly for any game summoning mechanic and it reads instantly as authentic.

entries.title: Circle of Solomon and Triangle of Solomon, *Lesser Key of Solomon*
entries.creator: Samuel Liddell MacGregor Mathers and Aleister Crowley
entries.summary:
The Circle of Solomon and Triangle of Solomon as rendered in the standard diagram from the Mathers–Crowley *Goetia* (1904) — shown together on a single plate, demonstrating their relational geometry.

## Iconography
The plate shows both ritual instruments to scale. The Circle of Solomon is the operator's domain: triple rings bearing divine names, a central pentagram, and four cross-guards at the quarters. The Triangle of Solomon sits adjacent — an equilateral figure with 'Anaphaxeton', 'Primeumaton', and 'Tetragrammaton' inscribed on its three sides, and the name 'Michael' written within. The spirit is commanded into the triangle; the operator works from within the circle.

## Significance
Together these two geometrical spaces constitute the entire spatial grammar of Goetic evocation: inside the circle is safety and divine authority; inside the triangle is compelled appearance. The arrangement encodes a theology of names — the Hebrew divine names function as legal instruments to enforce the spirit's attendance and prevent its causing harm. The *Lemegeton* specifies that the triangle should be placed nine feet north-east of the circle.

## For artists & game designers
This two-object diagram is the master template for summoning-system design: a safe zone for the caster and a contained zone for the entity. The inscriptions can be swapped for any in-world language system while preserving the structural logic.

entries.title: Magic Circle from an Early *Key of Solomon* Manuscript
entries.creator: Anonymous (English or Italian scribe)
entries.summary:
A conjuration circle from one of the earliest manuscript witnesses to the *Key of Solomon*, the foundational text of the Western grimoire tradition.

## Iconography
The circle is a compact schematic: concentric rings bearing divine names in Latin, a central pentagram or hexagram, and radial divisions marking the quarters. The lettering is a mixture of Latin and Hebraic-style script, reflecting the text's claims to Hebraic antiquity. The diagram is characteristic of the working-copy tradition — meant to be reproduced on the floor or on parchment, not to be admired as art.

## Significance
The *Clavicula Salomonis* ('Little Key of Solomon') exists in hundreds of manuscript copies across Europe, each slightly different, each claiming to transmit the authentic circles and pentacles by which Solomon commanded spirits. This example captures the circle in its raw manuscript state, before the regularising influence of the print tradition. The divine names inscribed — drawn from Hebrew scripture, angelology, and the *Shem ha-Mephorash* — constitute its operative force: they compel the spirit's attendance and guarantee the operator's protection.

## For artists & game designers
A rawer, more hand-drawn aesthetic than the printed Goetia diagrams — useful when a grimoire prop needs to look like a working copy rather than a typeset edition.

entries.title: Pentacle Diagram, *Clavicula Salomonis* (BL Oriental MS 14759)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Hebrew scribe)
entries.summary:
A pentacle diagram from a Hebrew manuscript of the *Clavicula Salomonis* held in the British Library (Oriental MS 14759, f. 35a).

## Iconography
The diagram presents a circular magical figure inscribed with Hebrew script — divine names, angel names, or phrases from the Psalms — arrayed within a geometric frame of nested rings or a star pattern. The Hebrew character gives this version of the circle a markedly different aesthetic from the Latin manuscripts: the script flows with calligraphic fluency, and the diagram breathes with a different visual grammar than its European counterparts.

## Significance
The Hebrew tradition of Solomonic magic is distinct from — and predates — the European Latin manuscripts. The *Clavicula* circulated in Hebrew and Judeo-Arabic versions long before it was absorbed into European grimoire culture; this British Library manuscript is a rare witness to that Eastern strand. The divine names inscribed belong to the same system as those in the Latin texts — Tetragrammaton, the seventy-two names, angelic designations — but are rendered with the authority of the original scriptural language.

## For artists & game designers
A reference for a 'Hebraic' aesthetic in magic circle design — the Hebrew script creates a very different visual texture from Gothic or Roman lettering, useful for distinguishing traditions.

entries.title: La Clavicure de Salomon Roy des Hebreux (Wellcome Collection)
entries.creator: Anonymous (French scribe)
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wellcome Collection / Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A page from *La Clavicure de Salomon Roy des Hebreux*, an 18th-century French manuscript of the *Key of Solomon* tradition held in the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The manuscript page shows the characteristic French presentation of Solomonic material: elegant penmanship, decorative borders, and a circular magical figure inscribed with divine names in Latin and pseudo-Hebrew characters. French manuscript grimoires of this period often show a polished, almost luxury-item quality compared to rougher English working copies — these were prestige objects as much as practical manuals.

## Significance
France was a major conduit for the *Clavicula Salomonis* tradition in the 17th and 18th centuries, and the Wellcome Collection holds several important French Solomonic manuscripts. The French title — *La Clavicure de Salomon Roy des Hebreux* — underscores the claim of Hebraic antiquity that legitimated the whole grimoire tradition. The Wellcome bequest (Sir Henry Wellcome, 1936) brought a remarkable assemblage of magical manuscripts into public accessibility.

## For artists & game designers
French 18th-century manuscript grimoires have a polished Rococo quality absent from their English and German counterparts — use this aesthetic for a 'noble's grimoire' prop distinct from rougher working copies.

entries.title: Magic Circle of the Hours, *Heptameron*
entries.creator: Peter de Abano (attrib.)
entries.summary:
The signature magic circle of the *Heptameron*, the influential treatise of angel magic attributed to Peter de Abano (1250–1316), first printed in 1496.

## Iconography
The circle is remarkable for its layered complexity: the innermost ring bears the operator's space, the second ring names the angels of the day and hour, the third inscribes the name of the current season and its ruling angel, and the outermost ring cycles through the names of the winds and cardinal directions. Unlike the Goetic circle, which focuses on constraining dangerous spirits, the *Heptameron* circle is oriented toward angelic beneficence — it is an invitation to divine attendants, not a prison for demons.

## Significance
The *Heptameron* (so called for its seven-day structure) bridged medieval learned astral magic and the emerging Solomonic grimoire tradition. Its approach — invoking angels through their proper hours, days, and seasons — is rooted in the Arabic-derived *picatrix* tradition and influenced Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia*. The circle's architecture of concentric annuli encoding celestial time became a template for many subsequent conjuration circles.

## For artists & game designers
The *Heptameron* circle encodes a cosmological clock — concentric rings as nested temporal systems. A rich template for any game calendar or astral-time mechanic expressed visually.

entries.title: Grand Pentacle of Solomon, 1547 French *Heptameron*
entries.creator: Anonymous (attributed to Peter de Abano)
entries.summary:
The *Grand Pentacle de Salomon* from the 1547 French edition of the *Heptameron*, the supreme protective talisman of the whole Solomonic system.

## Iconography
The Grand Pentacle is a six-pointed star (hexagram, the Star of David, called the *Seal of Solomon* in this context) inscribed within a circle, with divine names positioned at the points and within the interlocking triangles. The outer ring bears further names of God in Hebrew and Latin. Unlike the working circles used during conjuration, the Grand Pentacle is a concentrated talisman — an object or diagram to be carried or displayed as universal protection.

## Significance
The *Grand Pentacle* (sometimes called the *Hexagram of Solomon*) stands at the head of the *Clavicula* and *Heptameron* traditions as the master symbol whose presence compels the obedience of all spirits. It is simultaneously a seal, a circle, and a cosmogram — the divine names inscribed within it encode Solomon's authority over the whole spirit world. The 1547 French *Heptameron* is a landmark early printed edition that fixed many of the visual conventions inherited by later grimoires.

## For artists & game designers
The Grand Pentacle is the 'master key' of the Solomonic system — a hexagram-in-circle that outranks all other symbols. Use it as a universal access token or override command in any sigil-based game system.

entries.title: Pentacle from the *Heptameron*, 1565 Edition
entries.creator: Anonymous (attributed to Peter de Abano)
entries.summary:
A pentacle diagram from the 1565 printed edition of the *Heptameron*, showing the characteristic woodcut style of early modern grimoire illustration.

## Iconography
The pentacle is a compact circular diagram with concentric rings and internal divisions — a geometric figure inscribed with names of God, angels, and sacred words in a mixture of Latin and pseudo-Hebrew. The woodcut medium imposes a crisp, high-contrast aesthetic: bold outlines, crosshatched shading, and the slight roughness of the relief-printed line. This was the visual vocabulary that readers of popular magical books in the 16th century would have recognised as authoritative.

## Significance
The 1565 edition of the *Heptameron* (printed alongside Agrippa's *Occult Philosophy* in many cases) reached an enormous audience and fixed the visual vocabulary of the magical circle for subsequent generations. The woodcut pentacle, cheap to reproduce and widely circulated, became the template for countless manuscript copies made by readers who traced or reinterpreted the printed designs. This is the printed grimoire circle at the moment of its greatest cultural dissemination.

## For artists & game designers
Woodcut grimoire diagrams have a distinctive aesthetic — bold, graphic, high-contrast — that reads differently from the fluid lines of manuscript circles. Useful for printed spellbook props or a 'popular magic' visual register.

entries.title: Magic Circle, *Heptameron* (Variant Manuscript)
entries.creator: Pseudo-Peter de Abano
entries.summary:
A variant manuscript circle from the *Heptameron* tradition, showing the variability of the working diagram across manuscript copies.

## Iconography
The circle follows the general *Heptameron* structure — concentric rings bearing divine names and angelic designations — but shows the inevitable variations of manuscript transmission: different proportions, slightly different name placement, and the individual hand of the scribe. No two manuscript copies of a conjuration circle are exactly alike, and these variations are themselves a record of how magical knowledge circulated and mutated as it passed between hands.

## Significance
The *Heptameron* circle exists in dozens of manuscript variants, each a faithful-in-intent but variable-in-detail copy of the tradition. This variant illustrates the 'living manuscript' quality of magical texts: unlike printed books, each manuscript is an active intervention, with copyists correcting, updating, or elaborating the diagrams they transmit. The differences between variants are data about how the tradition was understood and modified at different times and places.

## For artists & game designers
Manuscript variants give a 'hand-copied' magic circle a plausible authenticity — slight imperfections, unusual proportions, marginal annotations all signal that this is a real working copy rather than a printed template.

entries.title: Sigillum Dei Aemeth (John Dee's Wax Disc, British Museum)
entries.creator: John Dee (designed); craftsman (executed)
entries.shelfmark: British Museum, Department of Britain, Europe and Prehistory
entries.rights: CC0. Photo: Vassil. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
John Dee's *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* — the Seal of God's Truth — the central instrument of his Enochian operations with Edward Kelley (1582–1589), photographed in the British Museum.

## Iconography
The wax disc (approximately 22 cm across) carries a bewilderingly complex diagram: seven concentric rings of Hebrew letters alternating with a heptagrammic star, forty-nine angelic names arranged in a precise geometric code, and the names of the seven archangels radiating from the centre. The disc was placed beneath Dee's 'shewstone' (crystal ball) and at the legs of the *Holy Table* during the seances at which Kelley received the Enochian revelations.

## Significance
The *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* is the culmination of the medieval circle tradition translated into an Elizabethan cosmological instrument. Dee based his design on the *Sigillum Dei* of the *Sworn Book of Honorius* (13th c.) — one of the most ambitious of medieval magic circles — and elaborated it through his angelic communications. Where Solomonic circles use divine names to compel spirits, Dee's *Sigillum* encodes an entire cosmology of angelic hierarchy. This British Museum object is one of the few surviving physical ritual instruments from a named magician's working practice.

## For artists & game designers
The *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* is the most complex single circle in the Western tradition — a full cosmological map compressed into a disc. Study it for a 'master-tier' ritual object whose reading requires specialist knowledge.

entries.title: The Holy Table, Enochian System (John Dee)
entries.creator: John Dee (designed); digital reconstruction
entries.summary:
The *Holy Table* (Tabula Sancta), John Dee's ritual working table for the Enochian operations — a diagram of the surface on which the scrying stone was placed during the angelic séances of 1582–1589.

## Iconography
The Table is a square divided into a nine-cell grid, each cell bearing a divine or angelic name derived from the Enochian communications. The *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* wax disc was placed at the centre and at the four legs; the crystal ball rested on the central sigil. The overall design was dictated to Dee by the angel Uriel through Kelley's visions and constitutes a complete spatial apparatus for establishing contact with the angelic hierarchy.

## Significance
Unlike the Solomonic circles designed to constrain spirits, Dee's *Holy Table* is an invitational instrument — a sanctified platform for angelic communication rather than a prison. It represents the translation of the medieval magic circle into a piece of furniture, part of a complete Enochian cosmological system that includes the *Sigillum*, the Watchtowers, and the Enochian language itself. The British Museum holds both the original *Sigillum* wax disc and Dee's Enochian diaries.

## For artists & game designers
The Holy Table is the magic circle's domestic cousin — a ritual surface rather than a floor diagram. Useful for a 'scholar's study' magic system where the working space is a piece of furniture rather than a chalked floor.

entries.title: First Pentacle of the Moon, *Clavicula Salomonis* (Sloane MS 3091)
entries.creator: Anonymous (English scribe); digital reconstruction by Fuzzypeg
entries.summary:
The First Pentacle of the Moon from Sloane MS 3091, a 17th-century English manuscript of the *Clavicula Salomonis* held in the British Library.

## Iconography
The pentacle is a circular diagram inscribed with a pair of angel names flanking a central figure — a stylised eye or sigil — enclosed by a ring of Hebrew text taken from Psalm 107 ('They that go down to the sea in ships'). The *Key of Solomon* prescribes this pentacle to serve at night and to discover hidden and secret things.

## Significance
Sloane MS 3091 is one of the most important English manuscripts of the *Clavicula Salomonis*, part of the extraordinary collection of magical and scientific manuscripts assembled by Sir Hans Sloane (1660–1753) and now forming part of the founding collection of the British Library. The lunar pentacles of the *Key of Solomon* are a suite of seven diagrams, each governing a different aspect of lunar magic: discovery of secrets, protection against night dangers, spirit evocation, and so forth. They represent the most 'scholastic' layer of Solomonic magic — celestial forces systematically harnessed through inscribed symbols.

## For artists & game designers
The moon pentacles have a distinctive compositional vocabulary — eye or crescent at the centre, angelic names at the compass points, Psalm verse in the outer ring — that makes them easily distinguishable from the planetary pentacles. Clean reference for a lunar magic tier in a multilevel system.

entries.title: Pentacles of Saturn (Plate II), *The Key of Solomon* (Mathers, 1888)
entries.creator: Samuel Liddell MacGregor Mathers (ed.); source manuscripts 17th c.
entries.repository: Published edition (Mathers, *The Key of Solomon the King*, 1888)
entries.summary:
The Saturnian pentacles from Plate II of S. L. MacGregor Mathers's landmark 1888 edition of *The Key of Solomon the King*, the first scholarly English translation of the *Clavicula*.

## Iconography
The plate presents two or three Saturnian pentacles — circular diagrams bearing planetary symbols, Hebrew divine names, angelic signatures, and scriptural verses related to Saturn's domain of time, death, darkness, and binding. The Saturn pentacles of the *Key* are among the most severe in the collection: they govern the restraint of enemies, the prevention of access, and the compulsion of spirits associated with the underworld.

## Significance
Mathers's 1888 edition of the *Key of Solomon* was a watershed event for the English-speaking occult revival: for the first time, the Solomonic pentacles were available in a scholarly printed edition based on the British Library manuscripts. The book fed the Golden Dawn's ritual system directly — Mathers was a founding member — and through them influenced every subsequent Western ceremonial magic revival. The Saturnian pentacles carry the grimoire's most severe operations: binding, constraining, and compelling.

## For artists & game designers
Saturn pentacles encode themes of time, death, binding, and the underworld. In a tiered magic system, Saturnian imagery signals high-risk, binding-oriented operations distinct from the more beneficent planetary tiers.

entries.title: Magic Seals, Harley MS 5596 (c. 1450)
entries.creator: Anonymous (English or continental scribe)
entries.summary:
A folio of magic seals from Harley MS 5596, a 15th-century British Library manuscript remarkable for its striking visual similarity to later Icelandic magical staves (*galdrastafir*).

## Iconography
The page shows a series of linear, angular symbols — branching, intersecting lines without accompanying circles — that resemble the runic-derived stave patterns documented in 17th-century Icelandic magical manuscripts. Unlike the circular pentacles of the Solomonic tradition, these are linear marks, drawn on a page rather than inscribed in a ritual circle.

## Significance
Harley MS 5596 is a minor but important witness to the breadth of medieval English magical diagram traditions. The visual similarity to Icelandic galdrastafir suggests either a shared origin in a common European magical stave tradition or a parallel development of linear seal-forms independently of the circular Solomonic system. The Harley Collection, formed by Robert Harley (d. 1724) and his son Edward, contains numerous magical manuscripts that remain understudied relative to the more glamorous Sloane and Lansdowne collections.

## For artists & game designers
Linear stave-forms are visually distinct from circular pentacles — they look like runes or structural schematics rather than celestial diagrams. Useful for a 'northern' or 'runic' magic tradition within a world that also contains Solomonic circles.

entries.title: Grimoire du Pape Honorius (allegedly 1760)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
The title page (or a key page) of the *Grimoire du Pape Honorius*, allegedly printed in Rome in 1760 — one of the most notorious and controversial works in the history of occult literature.

## Iconography
The page shows the characteristic layout of an 18th-century French popular grimoire: dense typeset text combined with printed magical diagrams — circles, sigils, or characters meant to be reproduced during the ritual operations. The title claims papal authority, a claim that simultaneously shocked Christian readers and inflated the text's perceived power.

## Significance
The *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* (not to be confused with the medieval *Sworn Book of Honorius*, though the confusion is deliberate) is a Catholic-inflected diabolical grimoire that combines standard Solomonic conjuration with elements of Black Mass iconography. Its claim to derive from Pope Honorius III gave it enormous notoriety; it circulated widely in France and Italy and was repeatedly condemned. The text exemplifies the 18th-century strand of anti-clerical black magic grimoires that parody Catholic ritual as a vehicle for diabolical operation. Owen Davies describes it as one of the most scandalous grimoires ever printed.

## For artists & game designers
The *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* is the archetype of the 'forbidden book' — a text whose title promises (false) religious authority for black magic. A reference for a corrupt-cleric antagonist or a forbidden library prop.

entries.title: The Magician Raising a Spirit, *Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (1825)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (published by Robert Cross Smith 'Raphael')
entries.repository: Published: *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (London, 1825)
entries.rights: Public domain (no known restrictions). Via Internet Archive / Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A plate from *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (London, 1825), one of the key popular occult compendiums of the early Victorian period, showing a magician raising a spirit within his protective circle.

## Iconography
The robed magician stands within a double circle inscribed with divine names, holding his wand aloft as a luminous spirit materialises in response to his conjuration. Around him are the standard props of Solomonic ceremonial magic: the ritual sword, the lamp, the inscribed parchments. The spirit appears not in a separate triangle but rising directly before the conjurer — a more dramatic theatrical arrangement than the strict triangle-of-art prescription.

## Significance
*The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century*, published by Robert Cross Smith ('Raphael'), packaged Solomonic and grimoire material for a Georgian popular audience hungry for occult knowledge in an accessible form. Its engravings — of robed conjurers, rising spirits, and smoking censers — were enormously influential on popular imagination and helped fix the romantic image of the ceremonial magician in the English-speaking world. The book is a direct ancestor of the Victorian occult revival that would culminate in the Golden Dawn.

## For artists & game designers
A complete 'summoning scene' reference from the popular imagination: robed figure, inscribed circle, materialising spirit. The composition is so archetypal it has become the baseline template for fantasy magic illustration.

entries.title: Conjuration Circle, *Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (1825)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (published by Robert Cross Smith 'Raphael')
entries.repository: Published: *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (London, 1825)
entries.rights: Public domain (no known restrictions). Via Internet Archive / Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A plate showing a conjuration circle diagram from *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (London, 1825), the popular occult compendium published by Robert Cross Smith ('Raphael').

## Iconography
The plate presents the circle itself as an object of study — a diagram of the operator's working space with its concentric rings, divine name inscriptions, cross-guards at the quarters, and the spatial arrangement of the ritual equipment. Unlike the scene-plates showing the magician at work, this is an instructional diagram: a template to be reproduced and worked within.

## Significance
The instructional circles of *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* were among the most widely circulated conjuration diagrams in early Victorian England, appearing in a book that was affordable, widely distributed, and aimed at a curious lay readership. They represent the moment at which Solomonic circles passed from manuscript transmission (copied hand to hand among practitioners) into print culture — available to anyone who could afford a popular occult compendium. This democratisation of the grimoire is a key moment in the history of Western magic.

## For artists & game designers
A printed instructional circle — cleaner and more regularised than manuscript working copies, but retaining the divine name inscriptions that signal authentic Solomonic material.

entries.title: Edward Kelly Invoking the Spirit of a Deceased Person (Ebenezer Sibly)
entries.creator: Engraved by Ames of Bristol (after Ebenezer Sibly)
entries.repository: Published: Ebenezer Sibly, *A New and Complete Illustration of the Occult Sciences* (c. 1790)
entries.summary:
Ebenezer Sibly's famous engraving of Edward Kelly (Kelley) invoking the spirit of a deceased person, from *A New and Complete Illustration of the Occult Sciences* (c. 1790) — the most influential visual representation of necromancy in British popular culture.

## Iconography
Kelley and a companion (traditionally identified as John Dee) stand within a glowing magic circle in a churchyard at night, holding wands and lanterns, as a swathed spirit rises from the ground before them. A corpse is visible in the background, and the scene is lit with a lurid supernal light. The circle is inscribed and appears luminous — a zone of safety amid the dangerous contact with the dead.

## Significance
Ebenezer Sibly (1751–1800) was an English physician and occultist who published his enormous encyclopaedic *Illustration of the Occult Sciences* (1787–1793) at the height of popular interest in magic and astrology. His engraving of Kelley's graveyard necromancy is the source image for virtually every subsequent popular depiction of the British necromancer: circle on the ground, wand in hand, corpse nearby, spirit rising. The image fused the respectable Dee-and-Kelley Elizabethan occult legacy with the lurid graveyard necromancy tradition to create an enduring national myth.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'necromancy scene' in the British tradition: circle, wand, corpse, ghost, night. Every element that makes necromancy visually legible is present and correctly staged.

entries.title: Edward Kelly Raising a Ghost
entries.creator: Robert Cross Smith 'Raphael' (publisher)
entries.repository: Published: Robert Cross Smith, *The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (London, 1825)
entries.summary:
A 19th-century engraving of Edward Kelly raising a ghost, published in Robert Cross Smith's *Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century* (1825) — a lighter, more theatrical variant of the Sibly necromancy scene.

## Iconography
Kelley stands in a magic circle holding his wand toward a luminous apparition — a ghost or shade rising at his command. The scene is set at night in a churchyard, the standard setting for necromantic operations in the English popular imagination. The circle glows with a contained, protective light.

## Significance
This image from Smith's compendium recycles and perpetuates the Sibly tradition of the necromancer Kelley, fixing the scene as a stock image of English magical practice. Edward Kelley (1555–1597), John Dee's scryer, became the iconic necromancer in English popular memory — partly because of Sibly's influential engraving, partly because of the genuine strangeness of his relationship with Dee and the Enochian angel communications. Both Sibly's and Smith's versions of the Kelly necromancy scene were reproduced and adapted throughout the 19th century.

## For artists & game designers
A companion to the Sibly engraving — simpler composition, same iconic elements. The Kelly necromancy images are the primary source for the visual grammar of 'Western necromancer plus magic circle'.

entries.title: Faust in His Study (Rembrandt, c. 1652)
entries.creator: Rembrandt van Rijn
entries.summary:
Rembrandt's famous etching (*c.* 1652) of a scholar in his study — traditionally called 'Doctor Faustus' or 'The Alchemist' — confronting a luminous magical disk inscribed with letters.

## Iconography
The scholar stands at his study window in the early morning, gazing at a glowing circular apparition that has materialised in the room. The disk bears a central monogram (INRI or AGLA) surrounded by a ring of Hebrew or Latin letters — a conjured emblem that appears to be the result of the figure's magical operations. His hands are raised in astonishment or invocation. The interplay of light — the dawn outside, the supernatural glow of the disk — is among Rembrandt's most refined chiaroscuro passages.

## Significance
The identification as 'Faust' is traditional but uncertain — the figure may be the alchemist or magus as generic type rather than the specific legend. What is certain is that Rembrandt has depicted the moment of magical appearance: the circle not as protective device but as apparition, materialising as a luminous, letter-inscribed disk in the practitioner's own space. The etching engages directly with the Paracelsian and Faustian imagery of the learned magician who summons and converses with spirits through inscribed symbols.

## For artists & game designers
Rembrandt's 'Faust' gives the magic circle its most sophisticated artistic treatment: not a floor diagram but an apparition, the circle itself as the spirit's body. A model for depicting conjuration as a scholarly encounter rather than a dramatic set-piece.

entries.title: Page from *Les Clavicules de R. Salomon* (Wellcome Collection, Vol. II)
entries.creator: Anonymous (French scribe)
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Via Wellcome Collection / Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A manuscript page from Volume II of *Les Clavicules de R. Salomon* (Wellcome Collection, MS L0073043) — a French 18th-century copy of the *Key of Solomon* tradition.

## Iconography
The folio shows the text of the *Clavicules* in elegant French handwriting, interspersed with diagrams or figures — magical characters, circles, or pentacle schematics woven into the instruction text. The French manuscript tradition of the *Clavicula* tends toward legible penmanship and decorated layouts, producing working books that are also objects of prestige.

## Significance
The Wellcome Collection's two-volume French *Clavicules de Salomon* represents the mainstream of 18th-century French occultism: the Solomonic tradition preserved in costly hand-copied manuscripts for aristocratic and bourgeois collectors as much as working magicians. Sir Henry Wellcome's collecting of such manuscripts — now accessible digitally through the Wellcome Library — has made this stratum of European occult culture newly visible to scholars. The *Clavicules* tradition in France produced some of the most elaborate and beautiful manuscript grimoires of the early modern period.

## For artists & game designers
A reference for the interior pages of a luxury grimoire — dense text and interspersed diagrams, in a calligraphic hand that signals education and refinement. The visual grammar of 'expensive forbidden knowledge'.

entries.title: La Danse du Sabbat (The Sabbath Dance Circle)
entries.creator: Emile Bayard (attrib.)
entries.summary:
*La Danse du Sabbat* — the Sabbath Dance — a 19th-century French illustration of witches dancing in a ring around a demonic figure, representing the folk-demonological tradition of the magic circle as collective ritual space.

## Iconography
Witches of both sexes dance in a circle around a central diabolical figure — a demonic animal or horned being — with their backs turned toward the centre in the characteristic posture described in early modern demonology. The scene is set at night outdoors, lit by the uncanny light of the sabbath fire or the demon's presence. The circle here is not inscribed on the ground but constituted by the dancers' bodies — the ring as living geometry.

## Significance
The witches' sabbath circle is the demonic inversion of the Solomonic protective circle: where the conjurer's circle excluded dangerous spirits, the sabbath circle enclosed them. The tradition of a circular dance at the sabbath derives from early modern demonological treatises — the *Malleus Maleficarum*, Jean Bodin's *De la démonomanie* — which described the sabbath in terms adapted from popular festive customs and then demonised. 19th-century French illustrators like Bayard gave this tradition a romantic-macabre aesthetic that fed the occult revival's imagery of witchcraft.

## For artists & game designers
The sabbath circle is the folk-magic alternative to the grimoire circle: not inscribed and geometric but constituted by bodies in motion. A visual counterpart to the scholarly Solomonic system — useful for a coven-based or populist magic tradition.
